0: Potential pathways to reduce environmental impact in a greenhouse tomato production: Life cycle assessment for different irrigation and fertilization treatments
Yaqiong Fan a, b, C , Zhaofu Luo b , Xinmei Hao , Sien Li b, , Shaozhong Kang b,

<list>a College of Water Resources Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi 030024, PR China 
b Center for Agricultural Water Research in China, College of Water Resources and Civil Engineering, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100083, PR China 
C National Field Scientific Observation and Research Station on Efficient Water Use of Oasis Agricultural Ecosystem, Wuwei, Gansu 733000, PR China</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Impact categories Environmental index Greenhouse construction Tomato production|Greenhouse vegetable cultivation with high productivity but also with high resource consumption has rapidly developed in Northern China. A comprehensive assessment of environmental impacts from the greenhouse production is vital to identify the important contribution processes and propose potential solutions to alleviate the environmental burden. A life cycle assessment (LCA) was used to examine the environmental impacts of greenhouse tomato production with the system boundary from greenhouse construction to tomato harvest. The impact of two fertilizers (organic and inorganic) under two irrigation levels was also compared. Nine impact categories including resource demand of energy consumption (EC) and water depletion (WD), pollution impact of global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (ACP), eutrophication potential (EUP), and photo- chemical oxidation (PHO), and toxicity impact of territory toxicity potential (TTP), freshwater toxicity potential (FTP), and human toxicity potential (HTP) were selected to calculate the total environmental index. The results showed that the greenhouse construction stage contributed to 85.1% of EC, 91.0% of GWP, 68.1% of ACP, 93.2% of EUP and 89.4% of PHO while the tomato production stage resulted in more than 97% of WD and toxicity impact. After normalizing and weighting, FTP was the most environmental risk with an environmental index of 0.106, followed by EUP and ACP. For different treatments, organic fertilizer (N1) with low irrigation level contributed to the largest ironmental index of 0.295 while inorganic fertilizer (N2) with regular irrigation level had the least. Organic fertilizer generally had lower environmental burden in all impact categories with an exception of the toxicity impact. Due to the stronger release of heavy metals, N1 had higher TTP, FTP and HTP than N2. Reducing heavy metals content in organic fertilizers, optimizing organic fertilizer application regime, extending the greenhouse life span, and recycling or reusing the construction materials are recommended to mitigate the environmental risks of solar greenhouse vegetable production.
1: Increasing demand for fresh vegetables and fruits has driven the rapid development of the greenhouse industry in northwest China, especially for vegetable production in recent years. Greenhouse culti- vation as protected horticulture could provide abundant, cheap, and high-quality products with high economic efficiency (Stanghellini et al., 2003). However, greenhouse production was perceived as a polluting activity due to high energy consumption (EC), waste generation, and a large amount of materials input. Comprehensive assessment of the

environmental impacts for greenhouse production is of great signifi- cance for accurately identifying and quantifying the most contributing components to the overall environmental impact during the production process, and for developing environment-friendly practices to mitigate the adverse impacts.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a valuable environmental manage- ment tool to provide assessments from a variety of decision criteria, such as cost and performance, which has been incorporated into the ISO environmental system in early 2000s (ISO, 2006), and widely used in agricultural settings (e.g. Bacenetti et al., 2016; Fusi et al., 2014; Wang

et al., 2007). In an assessment of the environmental impact of a product in its life cycle, LCA involves a thorough inventory of energy and ma- terials from 'cradle to grave' of the product or the process, such as resource extraction, production of materials, production of parts, and the production itself, distribution, usage and disposal, etc. Specifically, LCA could be divided into four steps: goal and scope definition, in- ventory analysis, impact assessment, and interpretation. In the goal and scope definition phase, the system under investigation and its function and boundaries are defined. In the inventory analysis phase, the resource consumption (inputs) and emissions (outputs) associated with the system are compiled. In the impact assessment phase, a variety of impact categories related to the study are selected, classified, and characterized. In the interpretation phase, the results from the inventory analysis and impact assessment phases were summarized, and conclu- sions and recommendations are provided based on the evaluation of the summarized results.

Up to now, most LCA studies on agricultural production have been used to document resource use and emissions for open field cultivation (Bacenetti et al., 2016; Brentrup et al., 2004a; Mungkung et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2014). Noya et al. (2015) used LCA methodology to compare the biomass yield and environmental impact profile of different cereal crops production and provide potential solutions to lower envi- ronmental impacts by considering different boundaries and production processes. Besides, LCA methodology has also been used to assess environmental impact for different protected vegetable production, such as tomato and cucumber (Antón et al., 2005; Cellura et al., 2012; Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). Previous studies on greenhouse tomato production with different system boundaries showed that reductions in the use of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides could lead to a positive shift of environmental effects (He et al., 2016). Therefore, the LCA applica- tion in the agro-food sector can help detect the environmental sensitivity of various processes and agricultural activities, and to provide infor- mation on identifying and optimizing some related processes and agri- cultural activities to minimize environmental impacts.

Energy consumption (EC), global warming potential (GWP), acidi- fication, eutrophication, and human toxicity are the main environ- mental impacts categories considered in previous studies on assessing environmental impacts from agricultural production processes. In recent years, comparisons of those impacts between conventional and organic agricultural systems have been gaining increasing attention (Amponsah et al., 2014; He et al., 2016; Hoeppner et al., 2006). Previous studies indicated that agricultural managements had great effect on those negative environmental impacts during the crop growth stage (He et al., 2016; Munoz et al., 2008), while fertilizers production, transportation, and application have consistently been identified as the greatest contributor to various categories of environmental impacts (e.g. EC, GWP, toxicity, acidification) (Bacenetti et al., 2016; Hasler et al., 2015). Meanwhile, organic fertilizer promoted as an environment-friendly alternative other than chemical fertilizer has been reported to result in lower EC, especially fossil resources, and less greenhouse gas emissions and eventually lower GWP (Hoeppner et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2010), but potential higher risks of eutrophication and soil toxicity. Khoshnevisan et al. (2014) reported that chemical fertilizers made a large contribution to the impact categories in tomato and cucumber cultivation. Pishgar-- Komleh et al. (2013) stated that chemical fertilizers were one of the important inputs to environmental impacts in cucumber production, and nitrogen fertilizer contributed to 81% of the total chemical fertilizer energy consumption. In apple cultivation, Longo et al. (2017) found that global energy requirement was 12% less, and GWP was 3% lower for organic than the conventional fertilizer production, while organic cultivation resulted in 3 and 2% higher human toxicity and photo- chemical ozone formation, respectively than conventional cultivation during the cultivation process. Zhu (2018) indicated that synthetic fertilizers and organic manure accounted for 48.7 and 59.4% energy depletion in conventional and organic system, respectively in apple production. He et al. (2016) reported that organic fertilizer application

had environmental benefits associated with a 54.9% lower environ- mental impact index compared to inorganic fertilizer production in greenhouse tomato cultivation. On the other hand, irrigation as an important agricultural management measure to crop growth is a noticeable contributor to the environmental burden, and the consump- tion of energy and emissions from the irrigation system should not be ignored when assessing environmental impacts of an irrigated system (Nunes et al., 2016; Pishgar-Komleh et al., 2013). In general, irrigation operation consumes a large amount of electricity, which is usually generated by non-renewable energy (Hokazono and Hayashi, 2012). Abeliotis et al. (2013) showed that electricity required for irrigation was the input dominating the impact categories in bean cultivation with the boundary from field preparation to harvest, as the generation of elec- tricity affects all the impacts associated with human or terrestrial and aquatic toxicity in addition to stratospheric ozone depletion, photo- chemical ozone formation and GWP. Besides, irrigation water applica- tion contributes to renewable resource consumption (i.e. water depletion) in crop production.

In addition, greenhouse construction materials such as rolled steel, cement, diesel use, etc., as well as labor work needed for the construc- tion and operation of the greenhouse all contribute to the environmental impacts, especially on EC. LCA performed in protected vegetable pro- duction, including melons, tomatoes, and cheer tomatoes indicated that greenhouse structures accounted for a substantial share (~30-50%) in the overall environmental impact of the system (Cellura et al., 2012). A study on glasshouse horticulture showed that EC accounted for about 75% of the total environmental impact and the contribution of glass- house structure was over 10% (Van Woerden, 2001). Torrellas et al. (2012) indicated that the structure accounted for 30-48% of the impacts of different impact categories in a multi-tunnel greenhouse tomato production.

Growing vegetables in solar greenhouse have become increasingly popular in northwest China, and tomato is one of the most popular greenhouse vegetables to satisfy vegetable demands and promote an economic gain in the region. Although the application of LCA in the agricultural sector has greatly increased over recent years, few studies have examined the environmental impacts of vegetable production system in a solar greenhouse and taken into account the construction of the solar greenhouse in the system. Intensive use of chemical fertilizers and irrigation is common in solar greenhouse vegetation production in the area, which has been rarely included in previous LCA studies. Meanwhile, emission data, especially GHGs emissions from soils during the plant growth period in previous studies were mostly from model simulation or taken from other settings. However, the output of an agriculture system including yield and emissions is highly variable depending upon climatic and soil conditions of the setting (e.g., tem- perature, rainfall, wind, soil texture and structure and soil pH) (Brentrup et al., 2004a, 2004b). Therefore, using such data from simu- lations from models without fully calibrated for the setting and from other settings might not produce reliable outcome on environmental impact assessment for an agricultural system. In this study, observed data of GHGs emissions and yield were used in LCA to assess the envi- ronmental impact of solar greenhouse tomato production in northwest China. The objectives were (i) to quantitatively characterize the envi- ronmental impact of solar greenhouse construction and tomato pro- duction stage in the solar greenhouse; (ii) to compare the overall and specific environmental burden of different fertilization and irrigation managements, (iii) to identify the most influential input and manage- ment practice that has the greatest environmental impact, and then provide potential solutions to mitigate the negative impact.

et al., 2007). In the inventory analysis phase, the resource consumption (inputs) and emissions (outputs) associated with the system are compiled. In the impact assessment phase, a variety of impact categories related to the study are selected, classified, and characterized. In the interpretation phase, the results from the inventory analysis and impact assessment phases were summarized, and conclu- sions and recommendations are provided based on the evaluation of the summarized results.

Noya et al. (2015) used LCA methodology to compare the biomass yield and environmental impact profile of different cereal crops production and provide potential solutions to lower envi- ronmental impacts by considering different boundaries and production processes. Besides, LCA methodology has also been used to assess environmental impact for different protected vegetable production, such as tomato and cucumber (Antón et al., 2005; Cellura et al., 2012; Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). Previous studies on greenhouse tomato production with different system boundaries showed that reductions in the use of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides could lead to a positive shift of environmental effects (He et al., 2016). Therefore, the LCA applica- tion in the agro-food sector can help detect the environmental sensitivity of various processes and agricultural activities, and to provide infor- mation on identifying and optimizing some related processes and agri- cultural activities to minimize environmental impacts.

Energy consumption (EC), global warming potential (GWP), acidi- fication, eutrophication, and human toxicity are the main environ- mental impacts categories considered in previous studies on assessing environmental impacts from agricultural production processes. Previous studies indicated that agricultural managements had great effect on those negative environmental impacts during the crop growth stage (He et al., 2016; Munoz et al., 2008), while fertilizers production, transportation, and application have consistently been identified as the greatest contributor to various categories of environmental impacts (e.g. EC, GWP, toxicity, acidification) (Bacenetti et al., 2016; Hasler et al., 2015). Meanwhile, organic fertilizer promoted as an environment-friendly alternative other than chemical fertilizer has been reported to result in lower EC, especially fossil resources, and less greenhouse gas emissions and eventually lower GWP (Hoeppner et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2010), but potential higher risks of eutrophication and soil toxicity. Khoshnevisan et al. (2014) reported that chemical fertilizers made a large contribution to the impact categories in tomato and cucumber cultivation. Pishgar-- Komleh et al. (2013) stated that chemical fertilizers were one of the important inputs to environmental impacts in cucumber production, and nitrogen fertilizer contributed to 81% of the total chemical fertilizer energy consumption. In apple cultivation, Longo et al. (2017) found that global energy requirement was 12% less, and GWP was 3% lower for organic than the conventional fertilizer production, while organic cultivation resulted in 3 and 2% higher human toxicity and photo- chemical ozone formation, respectively than conventional cultivation during the cultivation process. Zhu (2018) indicated that synthetic fertilizers and organic manure accounted for 48.7 and 59.4% energy depletion in conventional and organic system, respectively in apple production. He et al. (2016) reported that organic fertilizer application

had environmental benefits associated with a 54.9% lower environ- mental impact index compared to inorganic fertilizer production in greenhouse tomato cultivation. On the other hand, irrigation as an important agricultural management measure to crop growth is a noticeable contributor to the environmental burden, and the consump- tion of energy and emissions from the irrigation system should not be ignored when assessing environmental impacts of an irrigated system (Nunes et al., 2016; Pishgar-Komleh et al., 2013). In general, irrigation operation consumes a large amount of electricity, which is usually generated by non-renewable energy (Hokazono and Hayashi, 2012). Abeliotis et al. (2013) showed that electricity required for irrigation was the input dominating the impact categories in bean cultivation with the boundary from field preparation to harvest, as the generation of elec- tricity affects all the impacts associated with human or terrestrial and aquatic toxicity in addition to stratospheric ozone depletion, photo- chemical ozone formation and GWP. Besides, irrigation water applica- tion contributes to renewable resource consumption (i.e. water depletion) in crop production.

In addition, greenhouse construction materials such as rolled steel, cement, diesel use, etc., as well as labor work needed for the construc- tion and operation of the greenhouse all contribute to the environmental impacts, especially on EC. LCA performed in protected vegetable pro- duction, including melons, tomatoes, and cheer tomatoes indicated that greenhouse structures accounted for a substantial share (~30-50%) in the overall environmental impact of the system (Cellura et al., 2012). A study on glasshouse horticulture showed that EC accounted for about 75% of the total environmental impact and the contribution of glass- house structure was over 10% (Van Woerden, 2001). Torrellas et al. (2012) indicated that the structure accounted for 30-48% of the impacts of different impact categories in a multi-tunnel greenhouse tomato production.

8: The study was conducted at the National Field Scientific Observation

and Research Station on Efficient Water Use of Oasis Agriculture in Wuwei of Gansu Province (37°51'E, 102°52'N, 1581 m asl). The site has a typical arid continental temperate climate, with an annual average temperature of 8 °C, an annual precipitation of around 164 mm, and annual free water surface evaporation of 2000 mm. A typical solar greenhouse with brick and clay structures built in 2008 was selected for the study as a representative of protected agriculture in this area.
9: The LCA methodology was chosen to evaluate the environmental impact of tomato production in a solar greenhouse, which has been effectively used in agricultural systems over the decades. The procedure of a typical LCA outlined in the ISO standards (ISO, 2006) includes four phases: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assess- ment, and interpretation.
10: The goal of the study was to assess the environmental impact of to- mato production in a solar greenhouse in northwest China, involving different irrigation and fertilizer treatments. The function unit (FU) in LCA is the main function of the system and is taken as the reference unit for the inputs and outputs of the system. In this study, FU was defined as the production of 1 t of tomato per hectare per year. In the study, the tomato production system started from greenhouse construction and

ended in tomato harvest. Therefore, the processes considered in the analysis included the manufacture of solar greenhouse building mate- rials, transportation of materials, materials disposal, and agricultural materials, energy, and labor consumption in the tomato production stage (tillage, sowing, water, fertilizers, pesticides, and electricity con- sumption, etc.). Materials, energy and labor consumption during the growth period associated with tomato cultivation, such as planting, fertilization, irrigation, pesticide were included, but distribution and sale of tomatoes after the harvest were excluded. A flow diagram for the analyzed system is shown in Fig. 1.
11: The input and output of the system are shown in Fig. 1. The solar greenhouse with brick and clay structure used in the study was a typical one in the area. The structure of the solar greenhouse was shown in Fig. S1, and the actual cultivation area was 484.6 m² (76 m X 6.4 m). Information on the materials used and their transportation, and the construction of the solar greenhouse was derived from the interviews with the instructor of Shiyanghe station and workers who participated in the construction in 2008. Data on the amount of fossil energy, steel, brick, water depletion, electricity used in the construction was obtained and/or estimated according to building standards and referenced from the published literature of the time (Liu et al., 2010; Wu et al., 2010). The data regarding tomato cultivation was mainly based on the study conducted in 2016-2017 in the solar greenhouse. Details on the treat- ment setup and data collection could be found in Fan et al. (2020, 2021a, 2021b). Briefly, a tomato-cucumber-tomato rotation experiment was conducted with two N fertilizer sources (organic-N - N1, inorganic-N - N2) and two irrigation levels (regular - RI, low - LI). The mean tomato fresh yield for RIN1, RIN2, LIN1 and LIN2 was 147.4, 142.1, 130.7, and 125.3 t per hectare, respectively.

The amount of irrigation water, fertilizer, and pesticide use during tomato cultivation is shown in Table 2. The drip tape, electricity, and plastic film consumption during the growth period were also listed in Table 2. Besides, the materials and EC during the transportation of those materials from the manufacture (or distributor) to the site were expressed as diesel consumption in this study. More diesel fuel was consumed for transport of chemical fertilizer for N2 treatments than manure for N1 treatments due to a longer distance transportation for N2. The fermented cattle manure was applied as basal dressing through manual plowing for N1 treatments, while chemical fertilizer of N, P, and K in the form of urea, Ca(H2PO4).H2O, and H2SO4, respectively, mixed with irrigation water was delivered by drip tape for N2 treatments. 17: Environmental impact categories in the study were established based on the inventory analysis and are shown in Table 3. Main environmental

impact categories considered in the study were EC, water depletion (WD), GWP, acidification potential (ACP), eutrophication potential (EUP), photochemical oxidation (PHO), territory toxicity potential (TTP), freshwater toxicity potential (FTP), human toxicity potential (HTP), soot and dust (SD), and solid waste (SW).

Cumulative EC included the direct and indirect energy use, covering the impact from the production and transportation of main construction materials (steel, cement, plastic film, bamboo, etc.) and agricultural materials (fertilizer, plastic mulch, pesticides, etc.). The required cu- mulative energy consumption for each material in the study were extracted from reports of Chinese production standards, academic journals, and GaBi databases. The equivalent EC for the materials used in the analysis were shown in Table S1.

Water depletion included the impact from the greenhouse building stage in terms of structure combination and hydroelectric process, and tomatoes cultivation stage in related to irrigation and fertilization. De- tails on WD from different processes are shown in Tables 1 and 2.

Greenhouse gases emission including CO, CO2, CH4, NOx, SO2, and N2O is a large contributor to GWP, ACP and EUP. The total gas emission included the emission from both EC during the entire process, produc- tion and transportation of materials, and from the soil during the tomato growth period. The amount of gas emission for one unit of different input items in both greenhouse construction and tomato cultivation was obtained from previous study (Khoshnevisan et al., 2014) and GaBi databases, as shown in Table S1. Meanwhile, CH4 and N2O emission during the tomato growth period was obtained based on the measured data by Fan et al. (2021b). Ammonia volatilization and nitrate leaching were set as 23.77% and 12.50%, respectively, of the total nitrogen input

25: <header>2.2.4. Life cycle impact assessment and interpretation </header>
Indicators of each impact category were normalized and weighted. The normalization was computed using data from China's National

Bureau of Statistic (NBS, 1990 and 2000), which also widely employed by previous studies (Huijbregts et al., 2000; Liang et al., 2018). The weight values of indicators for each category were obtained from Liang et al. (2009) and Wang et al. (2007), which was established according to an extensive expert survey process in China. Then the results were further processed and interpreted for all environmental impact cate- gories based on the inventory analysis. Specifically, the interpretation of the results was undertaken by contribution analysis, which was per- formed to identify the different material inputs and managements hav- ing an important contribution to certain impact category.
27: The primary resources consumed in solar greenhouse construction and tomato production in the study included EC and water depletion. The EC was in the range of 2304.2-3072.0 MJ and water depletion ranged from 18.56 to 21.08 m³ yr for the four treatments (Table 4). With both greenhouse construction and tomato cultivation included, tomato cultivation contributed to less than 21% of EC while contributing to more than 97% of water depletion (Table 4). Specifically, rolled steel, cement, diesel, and fertilizer consumption accounted for about 87% of EC (Fig. 2). For different treatments, low irrigation level consumed 307.8 MJ yr ha higher EC than regular irrigation level, and the difference was 460.6 MJ when inorganic-N fertilizer compared to organic-N fertilizer application.
28: GWP, ACP, EUP and PHO for the four treatments in this study was 914.3 + 85.1 kg CO2 + 0.5 kg SO2 eq 2.1 + 0.2 kg PO2 and 0.11 + 0.01 kg ha-1 respectively (Table 4). Relative to tomato cultivation stage, solar greenhouse construction was the main process contributing to the four impacts, especially for GWP and PHO with the proportion from the solar greenhouse construction larger than 85%. Largely due to the great difference in yield, irrigation level was the main factor leading to the difference in ecosystem impacts between treatments during the solar greenhouse construction stage, while fertilizer type was the main factor during the tomato cultivation stage. Specifically, inorganic-N fertilizer brought about 4.0, 1.6, 3.1, and 1.6 times higher GWP, ACP, EUP, and PHO, respectively, than organic-N fertilizer treatments for the tomato cultivation stage. Among different sources, brick production and trans- portation contributed to the largest portion of the ecosystem impacts, leading to 55 + 3 kg CO2 eq yr-1 50 + 3 kg SO2 eq t-1, 79 + 3 kg PO2 eq yr ha-1 t and 58 + 1 kg C2H4 eq GWP, ACP, EUP and PHO, respectively. The other major sources were production, transportation, and use of rolled steel and cement for the construction of the greenhouse (Fig. 2).
29: The sum of TTP, FTP, and HTP was 15.9, 9.8, 15.9, and 9.9 kg 1,4- DCB eq yr-Superscript(1) ha-1 for RIN1, RIN2, LIN1 and LIN2, respectively (Table 4), and among the three pollutants, HTP was the main source of toxicity for both organic-N and inorganic-N treatments. The tomato cultivation stage accounted for more than 95% of total toxicity impact in this system. Organic-N fertilizer treatments had higher toxicity impact (~ 1-8 times greater) than inorganic-N fertilizer application. For organic system, manure application was the greatest contributor (>82%) to TTP and FTP. For the inorganic system, fertilizer was the major sources (>51%) contributing to TTP, and fertilizer and pesticide dominated (>99%) FTP. Electricity and fertilizer were the main source (>96%) for HTP for both organic and inorganic system.
30: Solar greenhouse construction and tomato production stage resulted in comparable SD emissions, i.e. 2.4 I 0.2 and 2.2 + 0.9 kg yr-1 ha t respectively. SW was almost exclusively from the solar greenhouse construction stage, and roller steel production was the dominant source. Among treatments, low irrigation level resulted in 392.4 kg higher SW than regular irrigation level treatments due to its lower yield.
31: The normalized values of environmental impacts for organic and inorganic greenhouse tomato production systems are shown in Table S3. Environmental index for the impact, calculated by multiplying the normalized value for each impact indicator by the corresponding weighting factor, was used to indicate the relative importance of the impact to the overall environmental impact among different impacts (Fig. 3). The total environmental impact index calculated as the sum of the environmental index of each impact was 0.28, 0.17, 0.30, and 0.19 for RIN1, RIN2, LIN1, and LIN2, respectively. In general, higher irriga- tion level led to lower environmental impact indices except for WD and HTP. The organic-N fertilizer application reduced EC, WD, GWP, ACP, and EUP while increasing the toxicity impacts of TTP, FTP, and HTP. In summary, organic-N fertilizer application led to 0.11 higher environ- mental impact than inorganic-N fertilizer application due to the much higher toxicity indices in N1 than N2 treatments.

Among the sources, fertilizer, brick, and pesticide were the most important components, accounting for 36.5 + 15.6%, 32.1 + 7.6%, and 12.1 + 2.9% of the total environmental impact index, respectively (Fig. 4). For the organic tomato cultivation system, the application of organic-N fertilizer (manure) dominated the total environmental index, accounting for 52.1% of the total environmental impact index, followed by the usage of brick. In the contrast, the usage of brick was the most important factor (39.7%) for the inorganic cultivation system and inorganic-N fertilizer accounted for 21.0% of the total environmental impact index. There was no evident difference in the contribution to the total environmental impact between two irrigation level treatments for each input.

33: 4.1. Contribution of environmental impact from different processes and inputs

Life cycle assessment is an important methodology to assess resource usage and emissions associated with a certain system, and to evaluate its potential environmental impacts. The methodology has been widely used in agricultural production systems over recent years (Bacenetti et al., 2016; Boone et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015). Most of those studies concentrated on a single impact index, such as EC, GHGs emission or GWP with relative narrow system boundary, such as from soil prepa- ration to crop harvest or plant cultivation to distribution (He et al., 2016; Jirapornvaree et al., 2021; Nunes et al., 2016; Pishgar-Komleh et al., 2013). In the present study, a more comprehensive assessment of the potential environmental impact of an agricultural system was conducted with environmental impact categories including EC, WD, GWP, ACP, EUP, PHO, TTP, FTP, HTP, SD and SW assessed. Further, a wider system boundary from greenhouse construction to tomato production was employed. Meanwhile, the observed GHGs emission from soils during tomato cultivation, as well as yield data were used in the study. Therefore, the results reported in the study were expected to be reliable.

In the study, FTP was identified as the most significant environ- mental index after the normalization and weighting process, followed by EUP and ACP for the greenhouse production system. Fertilizer applica- tion was the main source for FTP for all treatments, while the main source for EUP and ACP was from brick production and transportation. The results highlighted the importance of fertilizer management in the

drive to reduce the overall environmental impact in this system, as many other studies have stated. Liang et al. (2019) reported that N fertilizer contributed to well over half (63.3-73.1%) of the total environmental index in a cropping system including agricultural materials production and the arable farming system. Fusi et al. (2014) reported that the fertilization led to the main environmental load of rice cultivation, and the emissions associated with fertilizer application accounted for 86% of terrestrial acidification and ~75% of EUP. Similarly, other studies also confirmed that the input of fertilizer in the agriculture system was one of the main factors contributing to the emissions pollution, especially greenhouse gases (Iqbal et al., 2009; Nunes et al., 2016). Therefore, reducing N application and improving nitrogen use efficiency would be one of the vital ways to reduce the environmental burden of agricultural

systems. Another important factor contributing to the total environ- mental impact was materials consumption, especially for brick and rolled steel. Brick and steel consumptions were the major sources of environmental burdens in EC, GHGs emissions, and SD. Among the materials consumed, the use of brick contributed to the largest propor- tion to GWP, ACP, and EUP, which led to the high burden to overall environmental impact. A large amount of bricks usage was the charac- teristic of this solar greenhouse in the area. Therefore, using more environmentally friendly materials or recycling and reusing some con- struction materials in agricultural structures when possible are highly recommended to reduce the environmental burden of the agricultural system.

per year in the study was evaluated based on the life span of the greenhouse. As a result, extending the life span of the greenhouse could alleviate the environmental burden of greenhouse construction process when the impact was evaluated over the life span period. Similarly, Torrellas et al. (2012) proposed that extensions of greenhouse life spans were effective to reduce cumulative energy consumption in a LCA analysis for a multi-tunnel greenhouse.

In addition, GHGs emissions during the tomato growth period and pesticides use were also identified as the main contributors to the total environmental impact, which was in consistent with the study of Milà I Canals et al. Milà I Canals et al. (2006). Previous studies suggested that the efforts to decrease GHGs should be oriented to bettering manage- ment practices (Jiang et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2019; Mungkung et al., 2020). Adjusting the fertilizer source and dosage, irrigation level and frequency could be alternatives for decreasing GHGs emissions during the growth period. On the other hand, pesticide remains an important constraint on environmental health, which was consistent with other studies on agricultural systems with different system boundaries (Nunes et al., 2016; Pishgar-Komleh et al., 2013; Torrellas et al., 2012). Thus, the improvement in management practices should be oriented to improving fertilizer, water and pesticide use efficiency in agricultural systems.

Therefore, reducing N application and improving nitrogen use efficiency would be one of the vital ways to reduce the environmental burden of agricultural

systems. Another important factor contributing to the total environ- mental impact was materials consumption, especially for brick and rolled steel. Brick and steel consumptions were the major sources of environmental burdens in EC, GHGs emissions, and SD. Among the materials consumed, the use of brick contributed to the largest propor- tion to GWP, ACP, and EUP, which led to the high burden to overall environmental impact. A large amount of bricks usage was the charac- teristic of this solar greenhouse in the area. Therefore, using more environmentally friendly materials or recycling and reusing some con- struction materials in agricultural structures when possible are highly recommended to reduce the environmental burden of the agricultural system.
In the study, the environmental impact of greenhouse construction

per year in the study was evaluated based on the life span of the greenhouse. As a result, extending the life span of the greenhouse could alleviate the environmental burden of greenhouse construction process when the impact was evaluated over the life span period. Similarly, Torrellas et al. (2012) proposed that extensions of greenhouse life spans were effective to reduce cumulative energy consumption in a LCA analysis for a multi-tunnel greenhouse.

In addition, GHGs emissions during the tomato growth period and pesticides use were also identified as the main contributors to the total environmental impact, which was in consistent with the study of Milà I Canals et al. Milà I Canals et al. (2006). Previous studies suggested that the efforts to decrease GHGs should be oriented to bettering manage- ment practices (Jiang et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2019; Mungkung et al., 2020). Adjusting the fertilizer source and dosage, irrigation level and frequency could be alternatives for decreasing GHGs emissions during the growth period. On the other hand, pesticide remains an important constraint on environmental health, which was consistent with other studies on agricultural systems with different system boundaries (Nunes et al., 2016; Pishgar-Komleh et al., 2013; Torrellas et al., 2012). Thus, the improvement in management practices should be oriented to improving fertilizer, water and pesticide use efficiency in agricultural systems.
41: <header>4.2. The environmental impact of different treatments </header>

In this study, the evident differences of the environmental impacts between treatments were mainly caused by fertilizer source instead of irrigation level. Our LCA analysis revealed that there was a distinct difference in environmental burdens associated with a change from inorganic to organic greenhouse tomato cultivation. Overall, the organic system led to better environmental performance for most of the impact categories, compared to the conventional, although the organic system did have higher impacts in the impact categories of toxicity.

During the tomato cultivation stage, the environmental impact of EC, GWP, ACP, and EUP for N2 was 2.6, 4.0, 1.6, and 3.1 times that of organic fertilizer treatment, which was consistent with the previous studies (He et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2018). The non-renewable energy sources and GWP in the conventional farming system was found 69.5% and 79.4%, respectively higher, compared to the organic tomato pro- duction system (He et al., 2016). For EC, both the production and

transportation of nitrogen fertilizer consumed much higher energy than the organic fertilizer, as the delivery of organic manure was mostly from nearby farms to fields. Zhu et al. (2018) indicated that the energy resources/non-renewable material consumption was considerably dependent on the production and supply of chemical N and diesel in an apple production system. Greenhouse gases emission was the largest source for the overall GWP for both treatments in this study, while inorganic fertilizer had 5.5 times gases emissions of organic fertilizer treatment, mainly due to the higher N2O emission (Fan et al., 2021b). Inorganic treatments also brought the higher risk of ACP through increasing NH3 emission and soil mineral-N content, which were considered as the dominant factors for ACP (Brentrup et al., 2004b; He et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2018). The direct application of nitrogen for inorganic treatments could stimulate NH3 release through volatilization, and increase soil mineral-N content (Fan et al., 2020). In our study, NH3 release for the inorganic treatments contributed to 83% of ACP, and was 1.5 times that of organic treatments. Inorganic fertilizer application also had higher EUP value from larger SOx release in addition to NH3 and NO- release than organic cultivation.

On the contrast, organic treatments had larger impacts regarding all three toxicity indexes with TTP, FTP and HTP of 8.0, 2.8 and 1.1 times higher than the inorganic treatments. The greater toxicity potential was mainly attributed to the higher content of heavy metals found in manure used in organic treatments, as our data on toxicity were from the reports and academic journals. The toxicity level in the study was lower than that in the China National Standard for organic and inorganic fertilizers (GB18877-2002), which was formulated by the Chinese Ministry of Agriculture based on systematic investigation and analysis of and field experiments. As a result. the release rate of heavy metals from organic fertilizer was 58.2, 30.9, 5.6 and 18.7 times higher, respectively, for Cd, Cu, Pb and Zn content, compared to inorganic fertilizer. Then, organic fertilizer treatment greatly increased the risks of TTP and HTP in our study, which were consistent with previous studies by Longo et al. (2017) and Zhu et al. (2018). They both reported that the presence of large organic manure led to a gradual increase in soil toxicity risk by releasing heavy metals. He et al. (2016) found that organic fertilizer manure resulted in 1.25, 1.9, 1.43, and 1.6 times higher Cd, Pb, Cu, and Zn content in soils, respectively than inorganic fertilizer for the arable farming stage in an apple production system. Wang et al. (2014) also suggested that soil toxicity risks would be increased with rising organic

fertilizer input. Mohamad et al. (2014) indicated that the organic system with manure fertilization had a higher environmental impact on human health and ecosystem quality from a life cycle assessment on olive cultivation. On the other hand, the content of heavy metals such as Zn and Cu in cattle manures was extremely variable in China, depending on feed production processes, additives, local policy, etc. Actually, Zn content in cattle manure in China was reported in the range of 10.36 ~ 2229.08 mg kg with an average of 183.49 mg kg-1, while Cu content ranged 3.95 ~ 1257.13 mg kg-1 and mean of 69.85 mg kg (Xu et al., 2019). Therefore, the results on the toxicity impact and the total envi- ronmental index comparisons between organic and inorganic fertilizer could be reversed if the heavy metals content in organic fertilizer (manure) is within the low end of the range reported in Xu et al. (2019). In the paper, they also laid out possible ways to reduce heavy metal content of organic fertilizers. Therefore, reducing heavy metal content in organic manure is a key for organic greenhouse vegetable production in the area, and selecting appropriate organic fertilizer type and opti- mizing application amounts and frequency to reduce heavy metals release to soil and water are of particular importance.

45: In the study, life cycle assessment was conducted to explore the environmental impact from solar greenhouse tomato production, and the environmental index of both greenhouse construction stage and tomato production stage was evaluated. The impact of two fertilizing types (organic VS. inorganic) and two irrigation levels were compared. Our results showed that greenhouse construction stage contributed to most of EC and ecosystem impact categories of GWP, ACP, EUP and PHO, while tomato cultivation stage dominated the toxicity impact categories. Organic fertilizer application reduced the environmental impacts for all examined impact categories except for the toxicity impact, highlighting the major risk in manure application. Reducing heavy metal contents in organic fertilizers, improving fertilizer use ef- ficiency, increasing yield by optimizing nutrient and water application, adopting environmental-friendly and/or reusing and recycling green- house construction materials, and extending life span of greenhouses were presented as effective pathways to mitigate environmental burden of greenhouse vegetable cultivation system. Our results demonstrated that LCA can be used to identify the inputs having substantial contri- bution to environmental impact, and propose effective measures miti- gating environmental impact accordingly.
